title: Amnesty International flags child labor risks in Adani Power coal supply chain in Jharkhand
content: Amnesty International has flagged significant child labor risks in the coal mining supply chain serving Adani Power plants in Jharkhand. The human rights organization documented instances of underage workers at three subcontractor sites linked to coal transportation and ash handling operations. An estimated 152 million children globally remain in child labor, with India being a significant contributor especially in mining and construction sectors. Adani Power, which operates coal-fired power plants in Jharkhand and other states, sources coal from both Coal India subsidiaries and private mines where labor audits have historically been insufficient. The report calls for mandatory supply chain audits under BRSR Principle 5 (Human Rights) and GRI 408 (Child Labor) standards. SEBI has recently tightened ESG disclosure requirements for listed companies, making supply chain labor compliance a material risk for Large Cap companies. Adani Power faces potential penalties under the Child Labour Prohibition Act and reputational damage that could trigger ESG fund exclusion and MSCI downgrade. Industry experts estimate remediation costs of 20-50 Cr for supply chain restructuring and independent audit programs.
summary: Amnesty International documents child labor risks at coal subcontractor sites serving Adani Power in Jharkhand, calling for mandatory supply chain audits and BRSR compliance.
